commands.author: eabase
commands.command: please have a look, and find us some working instructions.
commands.freeform_prompt: Please have a look, and find us some working instructions.

target.title: Docs/Feature Request: Explain Realtime Talk voices, voice-agent role, and mobile/phone bridge options
target.body:
## Summary

Now that Realtime Talk is working, I love the feature. It feels genuinely useful and low-latency. A few user-facing docs and feature improvements would make it easier to understand and more practical while away from the desktop.

This is related to, but separate from, the OpenAI Platform API credit / Codex subscription quota confusion in #76498.

## Feature / UX requests

### 1. Clarify whether Codex/ChatGPT subscription entitlement can ever power Realtime Talk

If technically or business-wise possible, it would be wonderful if OpenAI Realtime Talk could use Codex/ChatGPT subscription entitlement instead of requiring separate OpenAI Platform API credits.

I understand this may not be possible because Realtime Talk uses the OpenAI Platform Realtime API path today. But from a user perspective, the split is surprising: normal OpenClaw chat can work through Codex OAuth, while OpenAI Realtime Talk requires separately funded API billing.

Even if this cannot change, a clear explanation would help.

### 2. Add phone/mobile audio bridge options

A phone-style entry point would be very useful. Examples:

- call into OpenClaw from a regular telephone line
- mobile audio bridge for Talk while away from the desktop
- possible integration guidance for FaceTime-style or system audio routes, if feasible

The practical use case is being able to talk to OpenClaw while mobile and keep project work moving throughout the day.

### 3. Document Realtime Talk voices and voice styles

More Talk documentation would help explain:

- available realtime voices
- which voices are supported by each realtime provider/model
- whether voices have stable style/accent behavior
- whether a voice can be instructed to use an accent or personality style
- how this differs from normal TTS voices used for non-realtime replies

For example, a user may see `alloy` in config but not know what other supported voices sound like or whether any are better suited for female, warmer, British, narrator, assistant, etc. styles.

### 4. Explain the realtime voice-agent mental model

Realtime Talk feels different from normal OpenClaw chat/TTS replies. It would help to document the architecture in user-facing language:

- what the realtime “voice agent” is
- how it differs from the normal/full OpenClaw agent
- when it answers directly
- when it consults the full OpenClaw agent
- why it may say something like “I’ll check with the agent”
- what context it does or does not automatically have

The feature is not broken; it is just surprising at first. A short mental-model note would make the experience feel less mysterious.

## Why this matters

Realtime Talk is exciting because it makes OpenClaw feel conversational and fast. The main gap is helping users understand:

- what powers it
- what it costs
- which voice options exist
- what the voice agent can and cannot do directly
- how to use it away from the desktop

That would make the feature easier to trust and adopt.
target.author: Hal2600

commands.reason: author association NONE and repository permission read are not allowed
